displayTitle: How X-rays Are Made (and Gammas Just Are)

beats.narration: Same photon, two birthplaces. X rays are made in a machine; gamma rays are born in a nucleus. That decides what you control.

payload.title: TWO BIRTHPLACES
payload.sub: The photon's birthplace decides which knobs you get.

beats.narration: The filament boils off electrons; kilovoltage slams them into tungsten. Braking electrons radiate X rays — about one percent of the energy. The rest is heat.
Two knobs: milliamperage sets how many electrons — intensity. Kilovoltage sets how hard they hit — penetration. And power off means beam off.

payload.heading: Two knobs — and an off switch

beats.narration: The tube emits a continuous spectrum — every energy up to the set kilovoltage. A decaying nucleus emits fixed lines: its own menu, forever.
So lock it in: k v is quality, m a is quantity — gamma has neither knob. You pick settings by picking the nuclide.

payload.sub: You pick gamma's settings when you pick the nuclide.
